paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1128 English Language — AI-authored Practice Paper 24: The Photograph on the MRT (Paper 2 §B style)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a Secondary 4 girl who takes a photograph for an elderly Indian auntie on the East-West Line one Sunday afternoon, and only later understands what the auntie was pointing at through the window. Section B [14 marks] — AI-authored practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1128/02 Section B format. Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. 10 short-answer items (Q1–Q10) on language use and inference, plus a 4-item flowchart MCQ (Q11) on the narrator's emotion arc, are included.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
It was a Sunday afternoon in November and the East-West Line was almost empty. I had boarded at Tampines and was heading west, with a paperback book on my lap that I was not really reading. The carriage rocked gently. Through the window, HDB blocks slid past, each one looking very much like the next.

Paragraph 2
An elderly Indian auntie stepped in at Bedok and sat down beside me. She wore a cream-coloured sari with a thin green border, and she carried a small black handbag that she kept on her knees. She had with her one of those old flip phones with worn keys that I had not seen in years. She seemed to be looking for something on it.

Paragraph 3
'Excuse me, girl,' she said after a while, in lightly-accented English. 'Can you help me to take one photograph?' She held the phone out shyly, the way you might offer something you were not sure was worth troubling someone over. I closed my book and said yes, of course, auntie.

Paragraph 4
She stood up, steadied herself on the metal pole, and pressed her left hand flat against the window of the moving train. With her right hand she pointed past the glass, urgently, as though afraid I would not catch what she was pointing at in time. 'There,' she said. 'There. There.' I lifted the phone and took the photograph. I took two more, just in case.

Paragraph 5
She sat down and thanked me. She looked at the small screen for a long time, much longer than the photographs themselves deserved. Then she smiled at me and put the phone away into her handbag. Eunos slid past. Then Kembangan. We sat quietly. I picked up my book again and pretended to read.

Paragraph 6
After she alighted at Paya Lebar, I turned to the window myself. I tried to remember which direction she had been pointing. Past the carriage, beyond a row of low warehouses, was a wide, cleared rectangle of red earth fenced off with green hoarding. Three cranes stood still against the sky. There was nothing else there — just dust, and the sharp afternoon sun.

Paragraph 7
It was only later that evening, scrolling through the news at home, that I understood. The cleared land I had passed was the site of an old row of two-room HDB flats that had been demolished the previous month under the Selective En bloc Redevelopment Scheme. The article showed a photograph: a low block, paint flaking, with bicycles leaning along the corridor and laundry strung between the windows. It had a kampung-like feeling, the writer said. A community of mostly older residents had lived there for over forty years.

Paragraph 8
I thought about the auntie pressing her hand against the glass. I thought about the urgency of her 'there, there, there', and the long quiet moment she had spent looking at the screen afterwards. I had taken a photograph of an empty field. She had taken a photograph of her front door, of the corridor where her neighbours had hung their washing, of forty years of an address that no longer existed. I wondered, sitting at my desk, how many other windows I had passed without seeing.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1128 §B style practice; not from a past examination paper. SERS / Selective En bloc Redevelopment Scheme: an HDB programme under which older blocks are demolished and residents resettled into newer flats; sari: a traditional Indian garment; alight: to get off a train or bus.)

questions.stem: Q1. In Paragraph 1, what does the phrase 'a paperback book on my lap that I was not really reading' suggest about the narrator? [1]
questions.answer: she was distracted or daydreaming, going through the motions of reading without really paying attention to the book or her surroundings

questions.stem: Q2(a). In Paragraph 2, what details suggest that the elderly Indian auntie is from an older generation? [1]
questions.answer: she wears a traditional sari, carries an old flip phone with worn keys that the narrator had not seen in years, and seems unfamiliar with finding things on the device

questions.stem: Q2(b). What does the word 'shyly' (Paragraph 3) reveal about how the auntie asked for help? [1]
questions.answer: she was hesitant or self-conscious about troubling a stranger, as though unsure whether her small request was worth the narrator's time

questions.stem: Q3. In Paragraph 4, why did the auntie point past the glass 'urgently, as though afraid I would not catch what she was pointing at in time'? [1]
questions.answer: the train was moving and the view she wanted to capture would only be visible for a few seconds — she was anxious that the narrator would miss the chance to photograph it

questions.stem: Q4. What is the effect of the narrator taking 'two more, just in case' (Paragraph 4)? [1]
questions.answer: it shows the narrator being kindly and thoughtful in a small practical way, even though she does not yet understand why the photograph matters so much to the auntie

questions.stem: Q5(a). In Paragraph 5, why did the auntie look at the small screen 'much longer than the photographs themselves deserved'? [1]
questions.answer: the photograph held an emotional meaning that went far beyond what was actually visible in the image, so she lingered over it as though seeing something the narrator could not see

questions.stem: Q5(b). Which phrase in Paragraph 5 echoes the narrator's distractedness from Paragraph 1? [1]
Q6. In Paragraph 6, what is suggested by the description of the cleared land — 'just dust, and the sharp afternoon sun'? [1]
questions.answer: to the narrator there was nothing to see there, no clue that anything had ever stood on the site — the emptiness is what struck her at first, before she understood its significance

questions.stem: Q7. In Paragraph 8, explain the contrast the narrator draws between her own photograph and the auntie's photograph. [1]
questions.answer: the narrator had only seen an empty field through the lens, while the auntie was photographing her former home, her neighbours' corridor and forty years of an address that had been demolished — they were looking at the same place but seeing entirely different things

questions.stem: Q8. What is the effect of the final sentence, 'I wondered, sitting at my desk, how many other windows I had passed without seeing' (Paragraph 8)? [1]
questions.answer: it suggests that the narrator now realises she has spent her life moving past places that mattered to others without noticing — the encounter has expanded her sense of what is worth paying attention to

questions.stem: Q9. In your own words, explain why the writer chose to describe the auntie pressing her 'hand flat against the window' (Paragraph 4) rather than simply pointing. [1]
questions.answer: the gesture is physical and intimate — pressing the hand against the glass suggests reaching towards something that can no longer be touched, which makes the moment more emotional than a simple point would be

questions.stem: Q10. What does the narrator's choice of the word 'urgency' (Paragraph 8) suggest about the importance of the photograph to the auntie? [1]
questions.answer: it suggests the photograph was not casual but pressing and emotionally significant — the auntie was racing against the speed of the train and perhaps against time itself to hold on to a fading memory

sections.instruction: Q11. The narrator's dominant feeling shifts across the Sunday afternoon and that evening at her desk. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best summarises her dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: helpful / curious / moved / reflective / amused / annoyed / proud / impatient. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ here so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'Photograph on the MRT' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings move through four stages — from a willing but unthinking helpfulness as she takes the photograph for the auntie, to a quiet curiosity about what the auntie had actually been pointing at, to being deeply moved as the meaning of the photograph becomes clear that evening, ending in a reflective awareness of all that she has previously failed to see. Pick the single best word from the bank for each section.

questions.stem: Q11(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 3–4 as she closes her book and lifts the phone to take the photograph for the auntie? [1]
Q11(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 6 as she turns to the window after the auntie has alighted and tries to remember which direction she had been pointing? [1]
Q11(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 7 as she reads the news article that evening and understands what the auntie had been photographing? [1]
Q11(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 8 as she sits at her desk wondering how many other windows she had passed without seeing? [1]
